essential_question: What's actually different between how you first learned this and what you know now?
objective: Reread the concept note and redo the Lesson Practice / Systematic Review pages for the one lesson (20–30) flagged as shaky, fixing the specific misconception rather than just the wrong answer.

content.heading: Rebuilt, not re-rolled
bullets: Yesterday you picked one lesson (20–30) to reteach.
Today: reread its concept note, then redo its Lesson Practice / Systematic Review pages.
The goal isn't a right answer — it's finding and fixing the actual misconception, before the Final.

content.heading: Go back to the source
content.note: Find your lesson's concept note: L20→W24-S1, L21→W25-S1, L22→W26-S1, L23→W27-S1, L24→W28-S1, L25→W29-S1, L26→W30-S1, L27→W31-S1, L28→W32-S1, L29→W33-S1, L30→W34-S1 (all under Curriculum/Geometry/Lessons/). Reread it slowly, and rewatch the matching Math-U-See DVD segment if the workbook alone isn't enough.

content.heading: Try the one matching your weak lesson — or all four for extra practice

content.preview: Session 3: reassess — a fresh mastery check across Lessons 20–30.

success_criteria.text: I can redo practice problems from my weak lesson correctly, using the concept note to fix the specific gap
I can describe what changed between my first attempt and this reteach — not just that I got it right, but why

questions.prompt: (L21 — 30-60-90) A 30-60-90 triangle has a short leg of 7. Find the long leg and hypotenuse.
questions.explain: Long leg = short leg×√3; hypotenuse = short leg×2.

questions.prompt: (L24 — Proofs) Two triangles share a side (reflexive), and two pairs of corresponding sides are marked congruent with no angle given. Which postulate applies — SSS, SAS, or neither?
questions.answer: SSS — three pairs of corresponding sides congruent (the two given pairs plus the shared reflexive side) is enough, no angle needed.
questions.explain: SSS only needs three side pairs; SAS needs an included angle between two given sides.

questions.prompt: (L27 — Similarity) Two triangles have two pairs of congruent angles. By which similarity postulate are they similar?
questions.explain: If two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another, the triangles are similar — the third angle pair is automatically equal too.

questions.prompt: (L28 — Transformations) Reflect the point (3, -5) over the x-axis. What are the new coordinates?
questions.explain: Reflecting over the x-axis keeps x the same and flips the sign of y.

questions.prompt: Reflect: which of the four redo problems matched your weak lesson? What's different this time versus when you first tried it?
